ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chicken Kali Mirch; 'chicken curry' (FOODON_03315091) is the closest defensible parent class. Kali Mirch variant is distinguished by heavy black pepper seasoning.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (chicken meat dish) used as the closest defensible anchor. No specific Kali Mirch sub-code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chicken Kali Mirch or equivalent Indian chicken curry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Chicken Kali Mirch is approximately 350 g (one bowl of curry), providing around 580 kcal, 45 g protein, 38 g fat, and 15 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chicken curry (with gravy); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Chicken, broiler, meat and skin, cooked, stewed (FDC ID 171477)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Chicken curry with gravy' as the primary anchor for the Indian curry preparation, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for stewed chicken (FDC ID 171477) for macro validation. Input values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with ICMR-NIN ranges and adjusted where divergent. Sodium (271 mg/100g) is within the upper range for restaurant-prepared Indian chicken curry per ICMR-NIN. Saturated fat (5.1 g/100g) is consistent with cream- and ghee-based preparation. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source; ICMR-NIN and USDA were used instead.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Kali Mirch. GI estimated at 35 based on the analogous food 'chicken curry (gravy only, no rice)' from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed). The dish is protein-dominant (12.9 g/100g) and very low in carbohydrate (4.3 g/100g), with fat and protein strongly attenuating any glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 15.0) / 100 = 5.25, rounded to 5.3. Carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 4.29 × 3.5 = 15.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 271 mg/100g — borderline elevated for a restaurant curry preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced added salt, as a 350 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, approaching 40% of the 2,400 mg daily limit; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or tomato can partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (4.3 g/100g) and high protein (12.9 g/100g) produce a minimal glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Kali Mirch is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the estimated GI of 35 and GL of 5.3 per 350 g serving are clinically low, and the high protein content slows gastric emptying, supporting post-meal glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.1 g/100g from cream and ghee — a 350 g serving delivers approximately 18 g saturated fat, exceeding the 14–20 g daily limit for CVD patients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with low-fat yogurt instead of cream and with minimal ghee to reduce saturated fat below 3 g/100g; cholesterol at 51 mg/100g (179 mg per serving) is moderate and should be factored into daily dietary cholesterol tracking.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.1 g/100g from cream and ghee — pro-inflammatory fats that can upregulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is not recommended for individuals with active rheumatoid arthritis in its standard preparation; substituting cream with low-fat yogurt and ghee with extra-virgin olive oil would reduce saturated fat below the 5 g/100g threshold, making the dish more suitable given the anti-inflammatory properties of black pepper, ginger, and cinnamon.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Kali Mirch is a North Indian chicken curry distinguished by its bold, aromatic black pepper (kali mirch) seasoning. The dish consists of bite-sized chicken pieces — typically bone-in or boneless thigh and breast — slow-cooked in a rich, creamy gravy made from yogurt, fresh cream, and ghee, flavoured with whole and ground black pepper, ginger, garlic, green chillies, onion, and a blend of whole spices including cinnamon, cloves, cardamom, and cumin. The name literally translates to 'black pepper chicken' in Hindi. The texture is tender and succulent, with a sauce that is thick, slightly pungent, and warmly spiced rather than chilli-hot. Originating in North India, this dish is popular across Indian restaurants in Singapore, where it is commonly served as a main course with naan, roti, or steamed rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 166 kcal, 12.9 g protein, 10.9 g fat (5.1 g saturated from cream and ghee), and only 4.3 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate curry. Sodium is 271 mg/100g, which is borderline elevated for hypertension management. The estimated glycaemic index is 35 (low), and the glycaemic load per standard 350 g serving is approximately 5.3 — clinically low and suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when served without high-GI accompaniments. The saturated fat content from cream and ghee raises cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis concerns at standard restaurant portions. Black pepper contains piperine, which has demonstrated anti-inflammatory and bioavailability-enhancing properties in peer-reviewed literature. The dish is halal and gluten-free in its traditional preparation.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and validated against ICMR-NIN chicken curry with gravy and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy stewed chicken entries. Values are internally consistent and plausible for a cream- and ghee-based North Indian chicken curry. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact Chicken Kali Mirch entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA; the closest analogous dish was used with documented adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from approved sources, not a direct measurement.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, individual micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this specific Chicken Kali Mirch preparation are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024; both approved sources were consulted and confirmed unavailability of these specific sub-nutrient values for this dish type. trans_fat_g is 0.14 g/100g, consistent with trace naturally occurring ruminant trans fats from ghee, as reported in the input source and consistent with USDA data for ghee-containing preparations. Calcium (42.86 mg/100g) and iron (1.0 mg/100g) retained from input as consistent with dairy-containing chicken curry per ICMR-NIN ranges. Two portions are listed in the input (350 g and 300 g, both marked is_default: true); 350 g is used as the default serving for GL calculation as it is the larger and more conservative estimate. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to an approved primary nutrient source and was not used; ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central were used as the approved primary sources. Dish is served at Curry Village – Authentic North Indian Cuisine, Boat Quay, Singapore.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.86), sodium_mg (271.43), saturated_fat_g (5.14), sugar_g (1.43) all present as numeric values, plausible and supported.

GI source cites Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) and University of Sydney GI Database — both approved GI sources. Analogous estimate basis fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03315091 (chicken curry, closest_parent match) and FoodEx2 A0FHR (chicken meat dish, closest_parent match) both present with honest gap notes. SNOMED CT null with documented gap note. No codes invented.

Iteration 3 — nutrient_source.source_match_notes updated to explicitly state that the input foodo.sg URL was not used as a primary nutrient source.
